observation_summary: The MonthDay class fails to perform arithmetic operations (plus/minus months/days) when the date is set to February 29th, throwing an IllegalFieldValueException because the code incorrectly attempts to validate the day-of-month against a non-leap year range (1-28) during intermediate calculations.
hypothesis: The existing implementation of add/plus/minus operations in MonthDay uses a generic approach that converts the partial date to a full instant (defaulting to a non-leap year) before performing arithmetic. This causes the validation logic to reject the valid February 29th date. The fix requires a specialized algorithm for MonthDay that handles month/day arithmetic without forcing the date into a non-leap year context.
prediction: The code will be updated to include a specific check for MonthDay objects. When adding months to a MonthDay, the logic will perform the month calculation directly on the month field rather than converting the entire partial to a full instant, thus avoiding the premature validation of the day-of-month field.
experiment_rationale: The fix diff shows the addition of a specific conditional block in BasicMonthOfYearDateTimeField that intercepts the operation when the partial is a MonthDay. This confirms that the original generic algorithm was insufficient for this specific data structure, necessitating a specialized procedural implementation.
reasoning_summary: The defect is a failure in the computational strategy used to perform date arithmetic on a partial date (MonthDay). The original algorithm incorrectly forced the partial into a full instant context, triggering an invalid boundary check. The fix introduces a new, correct algorithmic path for this specific case, which is a classic Algorithm/Method defect.

alternative_types.why_not_primary: While the failure manifests as an exception from a bounds check, the root cause is the incorrect algorithmic approach of converting the partial to a full instant, not a missing or incorrect guard condition.
The fix is a local procedural change to an existing method, not a design-level capability correction or the addition of a new class/object.
